turns.user_message: I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

step2_rag_chunks: If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

Patient: It would mean a lot to me, and to her too, I think. She's been asking me to quit for years, and it would be nice to finally be able to do it and feel like I'm doing something for her, for us. I don't want to keep disappointing her. But at the same time, I don't want to put so much pressure on myself that I end up giving up before I even start.

Therapist: The patient is saying that quitting smoking would mean a lot to him and his wife, and he's torn between not wanting to disappoint her and not wanting to put too much pressure on himself to succeed.
Can you imagine what your wife's reaction would be if you were able to make a genuine effort to quit smoking, even if you did slip up sometimes, but were trying your best to stay on track?

Patient: Yeah, I think she'd be really proud of me, even if I didn't quit completely. She'd see that I'm trying, and that I'm not giving up. And maybe that's what I need to focus on - not being perfect, but just trying my best.
Therapist: The patient is saying that his wife's pride and understanding would be a big motivator for him to try quitting smoking, even if he doesn't succeed perfectly.
What do you think would happen if you were to try a different approach this time, one that focuses on progress rather than perfection?

Patient: I think I'd be more open to it. If I'm not setting such high expectations for myself, maybe I'll feel more comfortable making mistakes and trying again. It's not like quitting smoking is going to happen overnight, right?
Current patient message:

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
